name: Croatia

colors.symbolism: from the historic arms of the Kingdom of Croatia and the red squares of the chequy shield
from the Croatian chequy and from the arms of Slavonia
from the historic arms of Slavonia and Dalmatia; the third pan-Slavic colour
appears in the regional crowns and on the Dalmatian leopards

description: Three equal horizontal stripes — red, white, and blue — with the Croatian coat of arms (a red-and-white chequy shield surmounted by a crown of five regional shields) centred over all three bands.
history: The pan-Slavic red-white-blue tricolour was first raised by Ban Josip Jelacic at his installation on 4 June 1848, drawing on the colours of the kingdoms of Croatia, Slavonia and Dalmatia. It served continuously as the Croatian flag through the Kingdom of Croatia-Slavonia (1868–1918), the Banovina of Croatia (1939–1941), the Independent State of Croatia (1941–1945) and the Socialist Republic of Croatia (1945–1990, with a red star in place of arms). The current design — designed by Miroslav Sutej with the new coat of arms — was adopted on 21 December 1990 by the Croatian Parliament, six months before the declaration of independence from Yugoslavia (25 June 1991).

features.symbolism: the pan-Slavic colours, combining the historic red-and-white of the Kingdom of Croatia, the blue-and-white of the Kingdom of Slavonia, and (with the blue) the Kingdom of Dalmatia

the red-and-white chequy shield (sahovnica) of Croatia, in use since at least the late 15th century, surmounted by a five-crown 'crown' formed from the historic arms of the Croatian regions: from hoist to fly, the oldest known arms of Croatia (a yellow six-pointed star above a silver crescent on blue), the arms of Dubrovnik, Dalmatia (three crowned leopards' heads on blue), Istria, and Slavonia (a marten between two rivers under a six-pointed star)
